# "Webster's American Dictionary of the English Language" is printed {#websters-american-dictionary-of-the-english-language-is-printed ._15il2ffb ._15il2ffa ._6y1v0z2 ._15il2ffl ._15il2ffj contentful-entry-id="74RcxE5DQmubhkgqT3YCTr" contentful-field-id="title" sentry-component="H1Xl" sentry-element="H" sentry-source-file="heading.tsx"}

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){.g1fwxf103 ._11axmyd9 ._11axmyd7 .fmx90r0}

[HISTORY.com Editors]{._198y7iph ._198y7ipc ._198y7ip4 .g1fwxfil .g1fwxfju .g1fwxfzw sentry-component="Box" sentry-element="Component" sentry-source-file="box.tsx"}

[Published: November 13, 2009]{._198y7iph ._198y7ipb ._198y7ip4 .g1fwxfil .g1fwxfju .g1fwxfzx sentry-component="Box" sentry-element="Component" sentry-source-file="box.tsx"}[![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){.g1fwxf103 ._11axmyd9 ._11axmyd1 .x4or4w6}Last Updated: May 27, 2025]{._198y7iph ._198y7ipb ._198y7ip4 .g1fwxflu .g1fwxfof ._6y1v0z8 .g1fwxfil .g1fwxfju .g1fwxfzw .g1fwxflu .g1fwxfoi .g1fwxfgu sentry-component="Box" sentry-element="Component" sentry-source-file="box.tsx"}

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){.g1fwxf103 ._11axmyd9 ._11axmyd3}

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){.g1fwxfzw ._11axmyd9 ._11axmyd3 .oj6ggc1}

Noah Webster, a Yale-educated lawyer with an avid interest in language and education, publishes his *American Dictionary of the English Language* on April 14, 1828.

Webster's dictionary was one of the first lexicons to include distinctly American words. The dictionary, which took him more than two decades to complete, introduced more than 10,000 "Americanisms." The introduction of a standard American dictionary helped standardize English spelling, a process that had started as early as 1473, when printer William Caxton published the first book printed in English. The rapid proliferation of printing and the development of dictionaries resulted in increasingly standardized spellings by the mid-17th century. Coincidentally, Samuel Johnson's Dictionary of the English Language was published almost exactly 63 years earlier, on April 15, 1755.

## Origins of Language {#origins-of-language ._15il2ffe ._15il2ffa ._15il2ffl ._15il2ffj sentry-component="H3" sentry-element="H" sentry-source-file="heading.tsx"}

[1775]{._198y7iph ._198y7ipd ._198y7ip6 .ayzlcr0 .g1fwxf3o .g1fwxf5i .g1fwxfil .g1fwxfju .g1fwxflc .g1fwxfl0 ._1cbvo06b .g1fwxfyc .g1fwxfzv sentry-component="Box" sentry-element="Component" sentry-source-file="box.tsx"}

First American abolition society founded in Philadelphia

The Society for the Relief of Free Negroes Unlawfully Held in Bondage, the first American society dedicated to the cause of abolition, is founded in Philadelphia on April 14, 1775. The society changes its name to the Pennsylvania Society for Promoting the Abolition of Slavery and the Relief of Free Negroes Unlawfully Held in Bondage \[...\]

[1865]{._198y7iph ._198y7ipd ._198y7ip6 .ayzlcr0 .g1fwxf3o .g1fwxf5i .g1fwxfil .g1fwxfju .g1fwxflc .g1fwxfl0 ._1cbvo06b .g1fwxfyc .g1fwxfzv sentry-component="Box" sentry-element="Component" sentry-source-file="box.tsx"}

John Wilkes Booth shoots Abraham Lincoln

President Abraham Lincoln is shot in the head at Ford's Theatre in Washington, D.C. on April 14, 1865 by assassin John Wilkes Booth. President Lincoln died the next day.

[1910]{._198y7iph ._198y7ipd ._198y7ip6 .ayzlcr0 .g1fwxf3o .g1fwxf5i .g1fwxfil .g1fwxfju .g1fwxflc .g1fwxfl0 ._1cbvo06b .g1fwxfyc .g1fwxfzv sentry-component="Box" sentry-element="Component" sentry-source-file="box.tsx"}

Taft becomes first U.S. president to throw out first pitch at MLB game

On April 14, 1910, President William Howard Taft becomes the first president to throw out the ceremonial first pitch at a Major League Baseball game. The historic toss on opening day is to star Walter Johnson, the Washington Senators' starting pitcher against the Philadelphia Athletics at National Park in the nation's capital.  Senators owner Clark \[...\]

American pilots engage in first dogfight over the western front

Six days after being assigned for the first time to the western front, two American pilots from the U.S. First Aero Squadron engage in America's first aerial dogfight with enemy aircraft. In a battle fought almost directly over the Allied Squadron Aerodome at Toul, France, U.S. fliers Douglas Campbell and Alan Winslow succeeded in shooting \[...\]

[1932]{._198y7iph ._198y7ipd ._198y7ip6 .ayzlcr0 .g1fwxf3o .g1fwxf5i .g1fwxfil .g1fwxfju .g1fwxflc .g1fwxfl0 ._1cbvo06b .g1fwxfyc .g1fwxfzv sentry-component="Box" sentry-element="Component" sentry-source-file="box.tsx"}

Loretta Lynn is born

Loretta Lynn, a singer who greatly expanded the opportunities for women in the male-dominated world of country-western music, is born in Butcher Hollow, Kentucky on April 14, 1932. Unlike some country-western stars that sang about a rural working class life but lived an urban middle class existence, Loretta Lynn's country roots were unquestionably authentic. Born Loretta \[...\]

[](/this-day-in-history/april-14/a-major-dust-bowl-storm-strikes){._24jwl20 ._1jnz0oc1y .g939qa4 .g939qa0 .g939qa1 .g1fwxflx sentry-component="Link" sentry-element="NextLink" sentry-source-file="link.tsx"}

[1935]{._198y7iph ._198y7ipd ._198y7ip6 .ayzlcr0 .g1fwxf3o .g1fwxf5i .g1fwxfil .g1fwxfju .g1fwxflc .g1fwxfl0 ._1cbvo06b .g1fwxfyc .g1fwxfzv sentry-component="Box" sentry-element="Component" sentry-source-file="box.tsx"}

"Black Sunday" Dust Bowl storm strikes

In what came to be known as "Black Sunday," one of the most devastating storms of the 1930s Dust Bowl era sweeps across the region on April 14, 1935. High winds kicked up clouds of millions of tons of dirt and dust so dense and dark that some eyewitnesses believed the world was coming to \[...\]

[![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){.g1fwxfzw ._11axmyd9 ._11axmyd1}1:40m read]{._198y7iph ._198y7ipb ._198y7ip4 .g1fwxflu .g1fwxfof .g1fwxfil .g1fwxfjy .g1fwxfzw sentry-component="Box" sentry-element="Component" sentry-source-file="box.tsx"}

[](/this-day-in-history/april-14/explosion-on-cargo-ship-rocks-bombay-india){._24jwl20 ._1jnz0oc1y .g939qa0 .g939qa2 .g1fwxflx sentry-component="Link" sentry-element="NextLink" sentry-source-file="link.tsx"}

[1944]{._198y7iph ._198y7ipd ._198y7ip6 .ayzlcr0 .g1fwxf3o .g1fwxf5i .g1fwxfil .g1fwxfju .g1fwxflc .g1fwxfl0 ._1cbvo06b .g1fwxfyc .g1fwxfzv sentry-component="Box" sentry-element="Component" sentry-source-file="box.tsx"}

Explosion on cargo ship rocks Bombay, India

The cargo ship Fort Stikine explodes in a berth in the docks of Bombay, India (now known as Mumbai), killing 1,300 people and injuring another 3,000 on April 14, 1944. As it occurred during World War II, some initially claimed that the massive explosion was caused by Japanese sabotage; in fact, it was a tragic \[...\]

[![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){.g1fwxfzw ._11axmyd9 ._11axmyd1}1:36m read]{._198y7iph ._198y7ipb ._198y7ip4 .g1fwxflu .g1fwxfof .g1fwxfil .g1fwxfjs .g1fwxfzw sentry-component="Box" sentry-element="Component" sentry-source-file="box.tsx"}

[](/this-day-in-history/april-14/montreal-canadiens-win-fifth-consecutive-stanley-cup){._24jwl20 ._1jnz0oc1y .g939qa4 .g939qa0 .g939qa1 .g1fwxflx sentry-component="Link" sentry-element="NextLink" sentry-source-file="link.tsx"}

[1960]{._198y7iph ._198y7ipd ._198y7ip6 .ayzlcr0 .g1fwxf3o .g1fwxf5i .g1fwxfil .g1fwxfju .g1fwxflc .g1fwxfl0 ._1cbvo06b .g1fwxfyc .g1fwxfzv sentry-component="Box" sentry-element="Component" sentry-source-file="box.tsx"}

Montreal Canadiens win fifth consecutive Stanley Cup

On April 14, 1960, the Montreal Canadiens defeat the Toronto Maple Leafs to win the Stanley Cup for a record fifth year in a row. The Canadiens reached the Stanley Cup Finals after sweeping the Chicago Blackhawks in four games, while the Maple Leafs defeated the Detroit Red Wings, four games to two. The championship \[...\]

[![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){.g1fwxfzw ._11axmyd9 ._11axmyd1}1:34m read]{._198y7iph ._198y7ipb ._198y7ip4 .g1fwxflu .g1fwxfof .g1fwxfil .g1fwxfjy .g1fwxfzw sentry-component="Box" sentry-element="Component" sentry-source-file="box.tsx"}

[1969]{._198y7iph ._198y7ipd ._198y7ip6 .ayzlcr0 .g1fwxf3o .g1fwxf5i .g1fwxfil .g1fwxfju .g1fwxflc .g1fwxfl0 ._1cbvo06b .g1fwxfyc .g1fwxfzv sentry-component="Box" sentry-element="Component" sentry-source-file="box.tsx"}

Katharine Hepburn and Barbra Streisand tie for Best Actress Oscar

On April 14, 1969, during the first internationally televised Oscars ceremony, Ingrid Bergman exclaims "It's a tie!" upon opening the Best Actress envelope---the first tie in a major acting category in three decades. The award went to both Katharine Hepburn, for her turn as Eleanor of Aquitaine in The Lion in Winter, and Barbra Streisand, \[...\]

U.S. bombs terrorist and military targets in Libya

On April 14, 1986, the United States launches air strikes against Libya in retaliation for the Libyan sponsorship of terrorism against American troops and citizens. The raid, which began shortly before 7 p.m. EST (2 a.m., April 15 in Libya), involved more than 100 U.S. Air Force and Navy aircraft, and was over within an \[...\]

Soviets agree to withdraw from Afghanistan

Representatives of the USSR, Afghanistan, the United States and Pakistan sign an agreement calling for the withdrawal of Soviet forces from Afghanistan. In exchange for an end to the disputed Soviet occupation, the United States agreed to end its arms support for the Afghan anti-Soviet factions, and Afghanistan and Pakistan agreed not to interfere in \[...\]

Greg Norman blows six-shot Masters lead in epic collapse

On April 14, 1996, third-round leader Greg Norman loses a six-shot lead in the final round of the Masters golf tournament and finishes second---one of the worst collapses in sports history. Nick Faldo wins the green jacket, finishing five strokes ahead of Norman. "I played like a bunch of \[expletive\]," the Australian tells reporters afterward. \[...\]

[](/articles/9-actors-who-won-an-oscar-playing-a-famous-historical-figure){._24jwl20 ._1jnz0oc1y .zu4ptk0 .g1fwxflr sentry-component="Link" sentry-element="NextLink" sentry-source-file="link.tsx"}

### 9 Actors Who Won an Oscar Playing a Famous Historical Figure {#actors-who-won-an-oscar-playing-a-famous-historical-figure ._15il2ffe ._15il2ffa ._15il2ffl ._15il2ffj sentry-component="H3" sentry-element="H" sentry-source-file="heading.tsx"}

Going back in time---to play a famous man or woman from history---was the ticket to Oscar gold for these nine men and women.
